(embedded source map)\n     ```\n   - There are no signs of dynamic code execution, obfuscated code, or embedded malicious payloads.\n   - The source map comment is a common artifact in transpiled or minified code, used for debugging.\n\n2. **Assessment of the Reports**:\n   - All five reports uniformly describe the code as re-exporting modules with an embedded source map.\n   - They consistently note the presence of a base64-encoded source map in a data URI, which is typical in build artifacts.\n   - No report indicates any suspicious or malicious behavior, such as network activity, code injection, or hardcoded secrets.\n   - The confidence levels are high (mostly 0.9 to 1), and they agree on the benign nature of the code.\n\n3. **Logical Flaws or Mistakes in the Reports**:\n   - The reports correctly identify the code's purpose and the benign nature of the source map.\n   - They emphasize that the embedded source map is standard and not malicious.\n   - No logical flaws or assumptions seem unwarranted; the assessments are consistent and cautious.\n\n4. **Scores Evaluation**:\n   - All reports assign malware scores of 0, which aligns with the absence of malicious activity.\n   - Security risk scores are very low (around 0.1), which is reasonable given the context.\n   - Obfuscated scores are 0, correctly indicating the code is not obfuscated.\n   - The risk scores are consistent with the analysis: minimal risk, no malware, no obfuscation.\n\n5. **Potential for Higher Risk or Malware**:\n   - Given the simplicity of the code, the absence of suspicious patterns, and the high confidence levels, raising the malware or risk scores is unwarranted.\n   - The embedded source map, while sometimes used to hide code details, here appears benign and standard.\n\n6. **Claims Based on Assumptions**:\n   - No report makes unwarranted assumptions;
